name: databricks
description: Query Databricks workspace compute, jobs, and SQL warehouses — list clusters and jobs, trigger job runs, and execute SQL statements

envVars.name: DATABRICKS_TOKEN
envVars.description: Databricks personal access token (generated under Settings > Developer > Access tokens)

envVars.name: DATABRICKS_HOST
envVars.description: Databricks workspace host, e.g. dbc-a1b2345c-d6e7.cloud.databricks.com or adb-1234567890123456.7.azuredatabricks.net (no protocol)

tools.name: List Clusters
tools.description: List all-purpose and job compute clusters in the workspace with their state and configuration

page_size.description: Maximum clusters to return per page (max 100)

page_token.description: Pagination token from a previous response (next_page_token)

historicalSummary.omitted: spark configuration, AWS/Azure attributes, and provider-specific payload fields

tools.name: List Jobs
tools.description: List jobs defined in the workspace, optionally filtered by name

name.description: Filter jobs by an exact (case-insensitive) job name

page_token.description: Pagination token from a previous response (next_page_token)

historicalSummary.omitted: full task definitions and provider-specific payload fields

tools.name: Run Job Now
tools.description: Trigger an immediate run of an existing job, optionally overriding job parameters

job_id.description: ID of the job to run

job_parameters.description: Job-level parameter overrides as a key-value map, e.g. {"date":"2026-06-11"}

idempotency_token.description: Optional token to guarantee the request triggers at most one run

tools.name: List Job Runs
tools.description: List recent job runs, optionally filtered to one job or to active/completed runs

job_id.description: Only return runs of this job

page_token.description: Pagination token from a previous response (next_page_token)

historicalSummary.omitted: task definitions, cluster specs, and provider-specific payload fields

tools.name: Cancel Job Run
tools.description: Cancel a job run; the cancellation happens asynchronously, so the run may still be running briefly

run_id.description: ID of the run to cancel (a no-op if the run is already in a terminal state)

tools.name: Get Job Run
tools.description: Get the status, timing, and task results of a job run (use the run_id returned by Run Job Now)

run_id.description: The canonical run ID to retrieve

tools.name: Execute SQL Statement
tools.description: Execute a SQL query against a SQL warehouse and return the result rows (queries; the warehouse enforces table permissions)

statement.description: SQL statement to execute, e.g. SELECT * FROM samples.nyctaxi.trips LIMIT 10

warehouse_id.description: ID of the SQL warehouse to run the statement on (from List SQL Warehouses)

catalog.description: Default catalog for unqualified table names

schema.description: Default schema for unqualified table names

wait_timeout.description: How long to wait synchronously for the result, between 5s and 50s, or 0s for async

row_limit.description: Maximum number of result rows to return

tools.name: Get SQL Statement
tools.description: Poll the status and result of a previously submitted SQL statement, e.g. after an async or timed-out execution

statementId.description: Statement ID returned by Execute SQL Statement

tools.name: List SQL Warehouses
tools.description: List SQL warehouses in the workspace to find a warehouse_id for executing SQL statements

historicalSummary.omitted: channel, tags, and provider-specific payload fields

prompts.title: Run a SQL query
prompts.prompt: List my Databricks SQL warehouses, then run a SQL query I provide against one of them and summarize the results.

prompts.title: Check job status
prompts.prompt: List my Databricks jobs and check the status of their most recent runs.

prompts.title: Cluster overview
prompts.prompt: Show me the clusters in my Databricks workspace and which ones are currently running.

SETUP_GUIDE.title: Databricks setup

steps.title: Get a workspace
steps.description: If you don't have one, sign up for the Databricks Free Edition at https://www.databricks.com/learn/free-edition, or use an existing workspace on AWS, Azure, or GCP.

steps.title: Find your workspace host
steps.description: Copy the hostname from your workspace URL, e.g. dbc-a1b2345c-d6e7.cloud.databricks.com (AWS/GCP) or adb-1234567890123456.7.azuredatabricks.net (Azure). Set it as DATABRICKS_HOST (no https://).

steps.title: Generate a personal access token
steps.description: In the workspace, click your username > Settings > Developer, click Manage next to Access tokens, then Generate new token. Name it, set a lifetime, and copy the token immediately.

steps.title: Store the credentials
steps.description: Add DATABRICKS_TOKEN=<your token> and DATABRICKS_HOST=<your workspace host> to your .env file.

steps.title: Verify access
steps.description: Run the List SQL Warehouses tool. A 401/403 means the token is invalid or expired, or token-based auth is disabled by your workspace admin.

notes: Personal access tokens are workspace-scoped — the token only works against the workspace host where it was created
Executing SQL requires a running (or auto-starting) SQL warehouse; the first query may take a minute while the warehouse starts
Databricks recommends OAuth over PATs for production use; PATs are the simplest option for getting started
Tokens can expire — if calls suddenly return 403, regenerate the token
